name: IRROPS Waiver Awareness — Use the Fee-Free Change Window
description: When weather, ATC, or operational events trigger a published airline travel waiver, you can change or cancel your ticket without fees — often without fare difference — even if your specific flight hasn't been touched yet. This skill teaches the agent how to find, cite, and exploit the waiver before the airline closes the window.

context.when_to_use: User has an upcoming flight in the next 1–7 days, AND there is a weather event, ATC delay program, IT outage, or operational disruption that might trigger or has triggered a published waiver at one of the involved airports. Also useful proactively — if a hurricane is forecast, the waiver often appears 24-48 hours BEFORE the storm.
preconditions: User knows the origin, destination, and date of travel.
User has a PNR and is logged in to the airline's app or website.
Internet access to check the airline's travel-alerts page.

principles: Waivers are published on each airline's website — usually at /travel/notices, /travel-alerts, or /flight-status. Find the page once, bookmark it for the future.
The waiver text always specifies: which airports/cities, which dates of original travel, which dates you can rebook to (the 'travel window'), and what's waived (change fee, fare difference, or both).
Self-serve via app first. Phone agents are often slammed during waivers; the app will execute a free change in 90 seconds. If the app can't, then call.
Many waivers allow you to fly EARLIER than originally booked — leaving a day ahead of a storm at no charge. Use this aggressively.
Waivers usually cover same city pair, but some let you reroute to a nearby airport (e.g. EWR ↔ JFK ↔ LGA, MIA ↔ FLL, IAD ↔ DCA).
Citations matter. Read the waiver number or the alert URL to the agent — 'I'm referencing your travel alert dated [date] for [airport]'.

phrases.opener: Hi — I'm looking at your travel alert for [airport/region] dated [date]. My ticket [PNR] is in the affected window. I'd like to rebook to [new flight] under the waiver.
phrases.cite_alert_directly: The alert says original travel between [date range], rebook by [date range], same origin/destination or [list of acceptable alternates], no change fee and no fare difference in same cabin. I want to use that.
phrases.self_serve_check: Before we get on the phone — let me try the app. The 'change flight' option usually shows the waiver automatically if you're inside the window.
phrases.ask_for_alt_airport: The waiver mentions co-terminals. Can I rebook from [origin] to [alt destination] like [airport pair]?
phrases.ask_for_earlier_flight: I'd like to fly out a day earlier than the storm. The waiver covers that, right?
phrases.graceful_close_success: Great — can you confirm the new flight numbers, new record locator, and email the itinerary? Same fare class, no fare difference?
phrases.graceful_close_failure: If the system isn't applying the waiver, can you have a supervisor force-apply it? The terms clearly cover my city pair and date.

tactics.name: Check the alert page before doing anything else
tactics.script: Go to: aa.com/i18n/travel-info/travel-alerts.jsp (AA), united.com/ual/en/us/fly/travel/notices.html (UA), delta.com/us/en/advisories/travel-advisories (DL), alaskaair.com/content/travel-info/advisories (AS), jetblue.com/at-the-airport/flight-disruptions (B6), southwest.com/travel-disruption (WN). Read the EXACT terms — dates, cities, alternate airports, fare cabin, expiry.

tactics.name: Try the app self-serve change first
tactics.when: Waiver is published and your ticket qualifies.
tactics.script: Open the app → My Trips → Change Flight. If the airline's IT systems are working, the waiver auto-applies inside the affected window. This is faster than a call and consumes no agent time.

tactics.name: Cite the alert by date and content
tactics.when: App didn't apply the waiver and you're on the phone.
tactics.script: Read the alert verbatim: 'Original travel between [date]–[date], rebook by [date], same origin/destination or [alt pair], no change fee no fare difference.' Agents have the same internal memo; reading it out shortcuts the conversation.

tactics.name: Move EARLIER, not just later
tactics.when: A storm or operational event is forecast.
tactics.script: Push the original travel forward by 24-48 hours to escape the storm window. Most waivers allow this. Beats spending a night at the airport.

tactics.name: Use co-terminal flexibility
tactics.when: Origin or destination has nearby airports.
tactics.script: Ask if the waiver covers co-terminals (EWR/JFK/LGA, IAD/DCA/BWI, MDW/ORD, OAK/SFO/SJC, MIA/FLL, HOU/IAH, DAL/DFW). Often yes, not always advertised.

tactics.name: Force-apply if the system pushes back
tactics.when: Agent insists there's a fare difference inside an explicit waiver.
tactics.script: Ask for the supervisor or 'have the override desk force-apply the waiver'. Internal codes exist (e.g. WAIVE/EX, OSI WAIVE) — agents know how but won't volunteer it.

boundaries: Do NOT claim a waiver applies if the airport, date range, or fare cabin doesn't match — agents check.
Do NOT pay a fare difference inside a waiver window without asking why; that's the most common 'mistake' agents make.
Do NOT change to a cabin higher than your original under a waiver expecting it free — most waivers are same-cabin.
Do NOT delay using the waiver. Waiver windows are short (often 7-14 days) and inventory drains fast.
success_signals: App auto-applies the waiver and produces a new boarding pass with no charge.
Agent acknowledges the waiver by name/date when you cite it.
Co-terminal or earlier-date rebooking is granted.
New PNR comes back with $0 collected.
failure_signals: Agent insists the waiver doesn't cover your origin even though it's listed.
System asks for a fare difference inside the waiver window.
Waiver mentions only 'change fee' waived but you're being charged fare difference for a same-cabin swap (challenge this — usually a misread).

exit_strategy.on_success: Get the new PNR, the new flight numbers, and screenshot the confirmation in case the change reverts (rare but happens during IRROPS).
exit_strategy.on_failure: Take screenshots of the published waiver page (the URL, the date stamp, the terms). File a written customer-service ticket citing the discrepancy — these get fixed by the back office reliably within 48 hours.
follow_ups: If you used the waiver to fly earlier, double-check checked-bag handling — bags sometimes get tagged for the original flight.
If the storm hits and your original flight cancels, you're already on a new itinerary — no further action needed.

required_user_info: PNR / record locator and flight number
Desired new date and time (a day earlier? a day later? same day different airport?)
URL of the airline's travel alert if user has already found it
